email_summary: Strauss placed a Q1 order of 30 MT standard grade cocoa at $2,780/ton with Golden Coast Cocoa Traders, confirmed via pro-forma invoice GCC-2026-0047. The shipment was delayed due to a vessel mechanical diversion to Las Palmas, arriving on March 6 — over three weeks past the original February 11-13 ETA, triggering the contract's late delivery penalty clause. Upon arrival, Strauss's QC found moisture content at 8.2% (avg), exceeding the 7.5% contract spec, while Golden Coast's pre-shipment SGS cert (January 24) showed 6.8%. Both the quality dispute and the delay penalty remain unresolved as of the last email on April 18, 2026.

contract_terms.payment_terms: Net 45 from bill of lading date
contract_terms.key_penalty: Late delivery beyond 7 calendar days past agreed window incurs 1.5% of shipment value per week delayed, capped at 12% of total shipment value.

open_issues.description: Strauss's incoming QC measured moisture at 8.2% average (max 9.1%), exceeding the contract spec of <7.5%; Golden Coast disputes this, citing their SGS Ghana pre-shipment cert showing 6.8%, and attributes the increase to the Las Palmas diversion. An independent third-party test (SGS Netherlands, split cost) has been proposed but not yet executed, and no resolution has been reached.
open_issues.source_ref: Emails 2026-03-08 (David Cohen), 2026-03-11 (Kwame Asante), 2026-03-19 (Kwame Asante), 2026-04-02 and 2026-04-18 (David Cohen); Contract STR-COCOA-2025-009 Quality Acceptance Spec and Penalty Clauses

open_issues.description: The shipment arrived on March 6, approximately 22 business days from the January 26 loading date versus the contracted 12 business days, exceeding the 7-calendar-day grace period and triggering the 1.5%/week late delivery penalty (capped at 12% of shipment value ~$10,008); Strauss flagged this on March 6 but no formal penalty credit or settlement has been agreed.
open_issues.source_ref: Emails 2026-03-06 (David Cohen), 2026-02-21 (Efua Mensah); Contract STR-COCOA-2025-009 Penalty Clauses

open_issues.description: Strauss's legal review of the risk-transfer clause (origin vs. destination) has been ongoing since early April; David Cohen promised a response by end of April but the last email on April 18 confirms no final position has been communicated, leaving Golden Coast's invoice on hold indefinitely.

commodity_benchmark: The ICCO cocoa spot price has risen sharply from approximately $3,245/ton in late March 2026 to $4,143/ton by mid-June 2026 — a ~28% increase over roughly 11 weeks — meaning the contract base price of $2,780/ton is now well below market, giving Golden Coast significant leverage on any price renegotiation ahead of the August 2026 renewal.
heads_up: Walking into this meeting, the single most critical issue is that two open liabilities — the quality dispute (potential price reduction under the independent-test resolution clause) and the late delivery penalty (up to 12% of shipment value, ~$10,008 on this order) — are both unresolved and intertwined; Strauss cannot finalise payment on GCC-2026-0047 without settling both, and Golden Coast has already flagged impatience (email April 14). Resolve the independent test appointment and the delay penalty calculation before the meeting to avoid Golden Coast using the payment delay as counter-leverage.
negotiation_brief: The supplier relationship is active and the contract (STR-COCOA-2025-009) runs to August 31, 2026, but it is under significant stress: two open disputes on the Q1 shipment — a quality non-conformance and a late delivery penalty — have stalled payment and eroded goodwill, with Golden Coast growing impatient. On price, the contract base of $2,780/ton is now deeply below the current market ($4,143/ton as of mid-June 2026), a ~49% gap that gives Golden Coast strong leverage in renewal negotiations and makes them unlikely to accept any downward price adjustment without a fight. Strauss's most important lever walking in is the contractual independent-test mechanism: if SGS Netherlands confirms moisture above 7.5%, the contract entitles Strauss to a proportional price reduction or full replacement — locking in that test promptly is both a financial safeguard and the prerequisite to paying the invoice and restoring the relationship before the August renewal window.

financial_signals.delivery_reliability_score: Poor — Q1 2026 shipment arrived ~3.5 weeks past the agreed 12-business-day window due to vessel mechanical diversion to Las Palmas; no prior on-time delivery data available in emails.

benchmark.source: ICE Cocoa Futures (CC=F) via Yahoo Finance

raw_emails.subject: Q1 Order — Golden Coast
raw_emails.body:
Hi Kwame,

Happy new year. We'd like to place our Q1 order — 30 tons standard grade. Can you confirm pricing and send a delivery schedule?

Thanks,
David

raw_emails.subject: RE: Q1 Order — Golden Coast
raw_emails.body:
David,

Happy new year. Q1 price is $2,780/ton. I'll copy Efua Mensah, our logistics coordinator, who will confirm the schedule and handle shipment documentation.

Shall I raise the pro-forma invoice?

Kwame

raw_emails.subject: Delivery schedule — Q1 Strauss order
raw_emails.body:
Hi David,

This is Efua from logistics. For your Q1 order of 30 MT, our standard CIF lead time from Tema port to Ashdod is 12 business days from vessel loading date. We are targeting a loading date of January 26, which gives an estimated arrival at Ashdod of around February 11-13.

Please confirm and Kwame will raise the pro-forma.

Efua

raw_emails.subject: RE: Q1 Order — confirmed
raw_emails.body:
Confirmed — 30 tons at $2,780/ton. January 26 loading, February 11-13 ETA works. Please raise the PFI.

David

raw_emails.subject: Pro-Forma Invoice — GCC-2026-0047
raw_emails.body:
David,

Please find attached PFI GCC-2026-0047 for 30 MT standard grade cocoa at USD 2,780/MT. Efua will coordinate the Tema port logistics from here.

Kwame

raw_emails.subject: RE: Pro-Forma Invoice — GCC-2026-0047
raw_emails.body:
Confirmed. Proceed with shipment per the PFI.

David

raw_emails.subject: Shipment update — GCC-2026-0047
raw_emails.body:
Hi Efua,

We are past the February 13 estimated arrival and have not received the shipment or a tracking update. Can you confirm current status?

David

raw_emails.subject: RE: Shipment update — GCC-2026-0047
raw_emails.body:
Hi David,

Apologies for the delay in responding. The vessel experienced a mechanical issue at sea and was diverted to Las Palmas for inspection and repair. We are now estimating 22 business days from the original loading date of January 26 — so revised ETA at Ashdod is approximately March 5-7.

The shipping line is MedLink and they are managing the situation. We will send the updated bill of lading once the vessel departs Las Palmas.

Efua

raw_emails.subject: Shipment arrived — quality inspection pending
raw_emails.body:
Efua, Kwame,

Shipment arrived this morning. Our QC team is conducting the incoming inspection now — will revert once complete. Note we are already 3+ weeks past the original 12-business-day delivery commitment, which under our contract triggers the delay penalty clause. We will address this separately.

David

raw_emails.subject: Quality inspection result — ISSUE
raw_emails.body:
Kwame,

Our QC has completed inspection on GCC-2026-0047 and we have a problem. Moisture content came in at 8.2% on average across the sampled bags (13 bags tested), with two bags as high as 9.1%. Our spec and your contract require less than 7.5% moisture. This is out of specification.

We need to discuss how you want to handle this — we are not in a position to accept this shipment at contract price. Please respond urgently.

David

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality inspection result — our position
raw_emails.body:
David,

Thank you for the update. I have reviewed this with our team.

I must respectfully push back on your findings. Our pre-shipment inspection at Tema on January 24 showed moisture at 6.8% — well within spec. A reading of 8.2% at destination is not consistent with what we shipped. Our position is that the moisture increase occurred during the Las Palmas diversion — extended transit in a non-standard hold environment over that period.

We shipped compliant product. We do not consider this a Golden Coast quality failure. We would request an independent third-party moisture test before any discussion of price adjustment.

Kwame

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality dispute — our position
raw_emails.body:
Kwame,

We understand your argument on transit conditions, but our contract is clear: risk passes at destination, not at origin. The product arrived out of spec. We do not have a copy of your January 24 pre-shipment moisture report — please send it.

We are open to an independent test but we need to agree on the lab and cost allocation first. We are using this batch under protest while we resolve the dispute. We will not pay full contract price for non-conforming goods.

David

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality dispute — pre-shipment certificate
raw_emails.body:
David,

Please see the attached pre-shipment moisture certificate from SGS Ghana (cert ref: SGS-GH-2026-00312, January 24). Result: 6.8% moisture, within spec.

We propose SGS Netherlands for the independent test, costs split equally. If the result confirms our position, we expect full payment at contract price plus the cost of the independent test.

Kwame

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality dispute — update
raw_emails.body:
Kwame,

We have reviewed the SGS Ghana certificate. We are not disputing the product may have been within spec at loading. But our receipt test governs under the contract — risk passes at destination. We are reviewing the contract language with our legal team.

On SGS Netherlands — we can agree to that lab but the cost split only covers the test cost, not broader liability conclusions. We will revert early next week.

David

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality dispute — still waiting
raw_emails.body:
David,

It has now been two weeks since your last message and we have not heard your final position on the independent test. We are holding the invoice pending resolution. Please advise — we cannot leave this open indefinitely.

Kwame

raw_emails.subject: RE: Quality dispute
raw_emails.body:
Kwame,

Apologies for the delay — this went to our legal team for review and has taken longer than expected. We are still finalising our position on the risk-transfer clause. I expect to have something for you by end of April.

David

raw_contracts.supplier_02_golden_coast.md:
# Contract Summary — Golden Coast Cocoa Traders

**Contract ID:** STR-COCOA-2025-009
**Supplier:** Golden Coast Cocoa Traders (Ghana)
**Category:** Cocoa — Standard grade beans
**Effective Date:** 2025-03-01
**Term:** 18 months
**Renewal Date:** 2026-08-31

## Payment Terms
Net 45 from bill of lading date.

## Volume Commitment
Minimum 25 tons/quarter, maximum 45 tons/quarter. Volumes below minimum for two consecutive quarters trigger a contract review clause.

## Pricing
Base price: $2,780/ton at signing, subject to quarterly review against ICCO composite benchmark. Either party may request a price review with 15 days written notice; agreed adjustments require written confirmation from both sides before taking effect.

## Quality Acceptance Spec
Grade A cocoa beans defined as: moisture content <7.5%, defect rate <5% by weight. Supplier bears the pre-shipment inspection obligation using an SGS-accredited inspection body at origin port. Strauss conducts incoming quality inspection at destination; in the event of a discrepancy between origin and destination test results, an independent third-party test (agreed lab, split cost) is the binding resolution mechanism.

## Penalty Clauses
- Late delivery beyond 7 calendar days past agreed delivery window: 1.5% of shipment value per week delayed, capped at 12% of total shipment value.
- Short-shipment (delivered quantity below confirmed PO quantity): credit issued on next invoice for the shortfall at contract price.
- Quality failure at destination (confirmed by independent test): price reduction proportional to degree of non-conformance, or full replacement at supplier option.

## Notes
Cross-reference against supplier 2's email thread: an unresolved quality dispute is open as of the most recent email. The January 2026 Q1 shipment arrived with moisture content measuring 8.2% at destination vs. the supplier's pre-shipment cert showing 6.8%. Golden Coast disputes the finding and attributes the moisture increase to the vessel diversion (Las Palmas). No independent test result is yet on file. The quality acceptance and risk-transfer clauses above are the key contract terms the prototype should surface alongside the open email thread — the question of whether risk passed at origin or destination is specifically relevant here.
